description: Array concatenation with + operator creates a new array and can cause performance issues with large files. Should use ArrayList or System.Collections.Generic.List for efficient insertion.
Random backup filename could potentially collide if script runs simultaneously. Consider using timestamp or GUID for better uniqueness.
Hardcoded API key 'sk-kritical-scx-local' exposed in source code, violating security best practices
Potential PATH corruption when removing binDir - doesn't handle empty PATH or trailing semicolons properly, could result in malformed PATH variable

line_hint: $ver = if($codex){ try { (& codex --version 2>&1 | Select-Object -First 1) } catch { 'unknown' } } else { '(not found)' }
description: Unsafe command execution - if codex command is malicious or compromised, this could execute arbitrary code
Get-NetTCPConnection may not be available on all systems (Windows Server Core, older Windows versions), could cause unexpected errors
Rollback version sanitization may produce invalid version strings by removing non-numeric characters, potentially causing npm install failures
Version sanitization regex may remove valid version components like pre-release tags (alpha, beta) or build metadata
Binary file content incorrectly converted to UTF-8 string which may corrupt the .app file during API upload
Multipart form data construction manually concatenates strings which may cause encoding issues and boundary format errors
Fallback to AadTenantId as API gateway URL is incorrect - AadTenantId is a GUID, not a URL
Potential command injection if $venvPy or $PSScriptRoot contain malicious values, though risk is limited in this context
SQL injection vulnerability if $t.tbl contains user-controlled input, though tables are hardcoded in this script
Potential file path traversal if key values contain malicious characters, though risk is limited by truncation to 24 chars
TestCmd execution may fail if binary requires interactive input or has different version command format, causing version detection to fail
Hardcoded path pattern for userLocal may not match actual installation locations for all package managers, potentially missing installed binaries
Hardcoded user-specific path that will fail for any user other than 'joshl', breaking portability
Assumes script is located exactly one level below repo root, may fail with incorrect directory structure
SilentlyContinue may mask critical errors like permission denied or invalid path
Error stream redirected to null may hide useful diagnostic information
Uses hardcoded Windows paths that won't work on macOS/Linux, limiting cross-platform compatibility
Remote script execution without validation exposes to MITM attacks and arbitrary code execution
Continuing on errors may mask critical failures during installation process
Suppressing output from external scripts hides potential errors and debugging information
Hardcoded user-specific path may not exist on other systems, causing failures
Potential SQL injection vulnerability - $q parameter is passed directly to sqlcmd without sanitization
Database name ($DbName) is directly interpolated into SQL schema without proper escaping, creating SQL injection risk
Temporary file creation and execution may fail if path contains special characters or if file permissions are restrictive
Hardcoded absolute path with specific username makes script non-portable and will fail for other users
Hardcoded Python executable path makes script non-portable and dependent on specific environment setup
Silencing sqlcmd errors could hide critical database connection or schema creation failures
Potential race condition if multiple instances run simultaneously with same timestamp
Hardcoded absolute path contains user-specific directory (C:\Users\joshl\OneDrive - Kritical Pty Ltd\Github\Kritical.SCXCode) which will fail for other users
Hardcoded absolute path to module contains user-specific directory that will fail for other users
Direct execution of sqlcmd without proper error handling - potential SQL injection if $Table contains malicious content
Temporary file creation without cleanup - potential accumulation of files in temp directory
Potential path collision if OutputRoot is at filesystem root (Split-Path -Parent returns $null), causing Join-Path failure.
Filename sanitization may produce duplicate filenames for different URLs (e.g., 'a/b' and 'a__b' both become 'a__b.md'), causing overwrites.
No timeout or retry mechanism for HTTP requests; transient network failures may prematurely halt crawling without recovery attempt.
File handle not explicitly closed, potential resource leak
Database connection not closed in exception path, potential resource leak
Exiting with code 0 on error, should use non-zero exit code for failures
Using 'replace' error handling may mask encoding issues and produce different content than original, leading to false hash mismatches
NVARCHAR conversion may alter content encoding/format compared to original UTF-8 bytes used for hashing during ingest
File handle not closed properly, potential resource leak
Hardcoded API key 'sk-kritical-scx-local' in source code
Arbitrary line count limits may exclude valid functions or include incomplete ones

line_hint: parsed = json.loads(mjson.group(0)) if mjson else {"purpose":"(unparseable)","identifiers_used":[],"bugs":[],"concerns":["model returned non-JSON"]}
description: No error handling for JSON parsing failures from regex extraction
Regex may match TypeScript keywords and non-identifiers like 'function' or 'return'
Hardcoded API key in source code (SCX-only violation)
No timeout handling for HTTP requests - script may hang indefinitely on network issues
File handle not explicitly closed - potential resource leak with many files
Database connection and cursor not closed in exception paths - resource leak risk
No validation of extracted JSON before loading - potential crash on malformed responses
Process started without proper error handling - if litellm fails to start or crashes immediately, the script will still create a PID file and claim success after health checks timeout
Recursive call in Heal mode could potentially cause infinite recursion if health check continues to fail
No validation that PID file contains a valid integer before casting to [int], could cause exceptions
Temporary PATH modification may not be reliable as environment variable changes don't affect parent processes
Hardcoded user-specific path that will fail for other users - should use environment variable or configurable path
Hardcoded absolute paths that may not exist on target systems - should be configurable or use relative paths
No timeout handling or error reporting if service fails to start within 40 seconds - should provide better feedback
Environment variable check could be simplified using a single method with fallback
Environment variable check uses $env:ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL instead of [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable for consistency, potentially missing user-scoped variables
Hardcoded Windows path separator may cause issues on non-Windows systems if script runs cross-platform
Hardcoded SQL Server connection string with default local server and no encryption (Encrypt=no) poses security risk if environment variable is not set
Hardcoded Windows path (C:\KriticalSCX\callback-debug.log) will fail on non-Windows systems and requires specific directory permissions
Database connection is opened and closed for every batch of rows instead of using connection pooling, which could cause performance issues under load
Environment variable retrieval only checks User scope, missing Machine and Process scopes which may contain the key
Potential timezone parsing issue with ISO 8601 format; cache.updated may not parse correctly in all locales
Missing -ErrorAction Stop in Invoke-WebRequest call; non-terminating errors might not be caught
No error handling for HTTP failures - script will crash on non-200 responses without meaningful error context
Hardcoded API key in source code - should be parameterized or retrieved from secure storage

line_hint: $items = @($Shards | ForEach-Object { if (Test-Path $_ -PathType Leaf) { [pscustomobject]@{ ref=(Split-Path $_ -Leaf); text=(Get-Content $_ -Raw) } } else { [pscustomobject]@{ ref='inline'; text=$_ } } })
description: Potential file path injection if shards contain malicious paths; Test-Path should validate paths more strictly

line_hint: $body = @{ model=$m; max_tokens=220; temperature=0; messages=@(@{role='user';content="Task: $t`n`nSummarise the SOURCE below for that task in <=100 words; keep concrete facts/names. SOURCE:`n`n$($it.text)"}) } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 6
description: No input sanitization for $it.text which could contain special characters that break JSON formatting
Temporary file creation with Get-Random is not cryptographically secure and could be predictable
No validation that $tmp file exists before passing to Python script, could cause script failure
File handle not explicitly closed, potential resource leak
Potential SQL injection if content contains malicious SQL code when cast to NVARCHAR
Database connection close not in finally block, may not execute on exception

line_hint: Suspend-KritOneDrive: Force flag ignored – both branches call Stop-Process -Force
description: The $Force switch is intended to control whether OneDrive is force‑killed after the graceful shutdown timeout, but the code calls Stop-Process -Force in both the if and else blocks, making the switch ineffective and potentially killing OneDrive even when the user only wanted a graceful stop.

If Get‑KritOneDriveExe cannot locate OneDrive.exe it returns $null; invoking '& $null /shutdown' throws a terminating error, causing the function to fail unexpectedly instead of handling the missing executable gracefully.

The -N argument is passed as the literal string "\"\"", which sets a non‑empty passphrase of two double‑quote characters instead of an empty passphrase. This prevents the key from being used without a password and can cause authentication failures.

The function returns the public key unconditionally after invoking ssh-keygen, but does not verify that $Pub was actually created. If key generation fails (e.g., ssh-keygen not found or error), Get-Content throws a terminating error, crashing the caller.

The code assumes ssh-keyscan is present and succeeds. If ssh-keyscan is missing or fails, nothing is added to known_hosts, leading to later SSH connections failing with host‑key verification errors. No error handling or fallback is provided.

The GitHub key ID ($id) is taken directly from the API response without validation. If the API returns an unexpected value (e.g., null, non‑numeric string), the DELETE request may be malformed or inadvertently target a wrong resource.

The function never emits or returns any result (no Write‑Output, return statement, or PassThru handling), so callers always receive $null and the documented HR27 logging, SessionId, Wave, and NoLog switches are never exercised.
Several parameters ($PassThru, $NoLog, $Wave, $SessionId) are parsed but never used, leading to silent failures and confusing API surface.
A Stopwatch is started and stopped but its elapsed time is never reported or logged, wasting resources.
Calls the base‑module command via '& $mainMod @params'; if $mainMod is a CommandInfo object this may fail—using the command name or scriptblock is safer.
HTTP header key 'content-type' is lower‑cased; while PowerShell tolerates it, the canonical header name is 'Content-Type'.
Token approximation uses $Chunk.Length instead of the trimmed string, slightly over‑estimating token count for trailing whitespace.
Banner loading trusts user‑controlled OneDrive paths without validation, opening a potential path‑injection or information‑leak vector if environment variables are manipulated.
Repeatedly attempts to Import‑Module from a relative path on each call, which can cause unnecessary module reloads and performance overhead.

The New-KritScxEmbedding function is incomplete: the Invoke-RestMethod call lacks required -Headers and -Body arguments, the function never returns a value, and the closing brace for the function is missing, causing a syntax error that prevents the module from loading.

If no SCX_API_KEY is set in HKCU, $currentKey is $null; calling .Substring on $null throws a NullReferenceException, breaking key‑switching and any caller that invokes this function without a pre‑existing key.
The default SecretsDir path is hard‑coded to a specific user’s OneDrive location, leaking internal directory structure and making the function unusable on other machines unless overridden.
The response stream is read via a StreamReader without disposing it, potentially leaking file handles under high error volume.

When an exception does not contain a TargetObject (e.g., network timeout), $lastErr is $null; accessing .IsRateLimit on $null is safe in PowerShell but makes the logic ambiguous and could mask unexpected errors.
Category name is interpolated directly into a file path without sanitisation, allowing path‑traversal (e.g. "..\\..\\secret") to write outside the intended documentation folder.

If the function later builds SQL statements using the $Model, $Provider, $Content or other user‑supplied strings without proper parameterisation, it creates a SQL injection surface for the optional EmitToDb path.
Reads entire JSONL files into memory with Get‑Content -Raw for each search, which can cause out‑of‑memory crashes on large log files.
When $env:KRITICAL_DECISION_LOG_ROOT is set to a non‑existent path the function silently falls back to directory walking, potentially hiding misconfiguration and making debugging harder.
Initial duplicate count is set to 2 (original + duplicate) even if the original entry was never logged in the current counter file, leading to an inaccurate count for the first duplicate.
Uses MD5 for token hashing, which is cryptographically broken; while acceptable for SimHash, it may raise security‑review concerns.

The _keyRotation variable is defined but never used; scxPost always uses the primary API key, ignoring the list of rotated keys (apiKeys) and thus breaking the intended key rotation and fallback mechanism.

Path detection treats any string containing '/' or '\' as a path and only checks existence; relative paths like 'bin/claude' will be considered a path, and if they don't exist the function falls back to Windows candidate search, potentially returning an incorrect executable path and causing spawn failures.

The replacement only matches the exact string '${env:SCX_API_KEY}'. If the configuration default includes whitespace or a different env variable syntax, the environment variable will not be substituted, resulting in an empty API key and runtime errors.
If both USERPROFILE and HOME are undefined, cfgPath becomes '.codex/config.toml' relative to the current working directory, which may unintentionally read a file from an unexpected location.

Read‑only validation may falsely reject legitimate SELECT statements that contain forbidden keywords inside string literals (e.g., SELECT 'insert') because it scans the whole lowered query for substrings like " insert ". This can cause unnecessary refusals.

The SQL constructed for the Lens catalog uses an IF … SELECT … ELSE SELECT … pattern that may be syntactically invalid on some SQL Server configurations, causing a runtime error when the tool is invoked.
Error messages from caught exceptions are returned directly to the caller, potentially leaking internal exception details or stack traces.
Calls .ToLower() on $m.stacks.core which is an array; PowerShell throws a MethodNotFound error, aborting the test suite.
Hard‑coded bearer token is a secret that should not be embedded in source code.
Select‑String is invoked without verifying the file exists; if the wrapper file is missing the cmdlet throws a terminating error, breaking the script.
Assumes the auth.json file exists; if it is absent Get-Content raises a non‑terminating error that later dereferences $j, potentially causing a null‑reference failure.

The '*' operator on a string produces an array of repeated strings, not a concatenated string. Passing this array to Test‑KritScxNaturalTerminator (which expects a single string) can cause type‑conversion errors or unintended truthy evaluation, leading to test failures or runtime exceptions.

The test manipulates an internal module variable to reset banner state. This creates a brittle dependency on the module's implementation detail; if the variable name changes or is removed, the test will break even though the module functionality is correct.

The script calls [System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseFile on $mux without first verifying that the file exists; if the path is wrong or the file is missing, ParseFile throws an exception and the script crashes

Session ID ($sess) is interpolated directly into raw SQL strings passed to sqlcmd, creating a SQL‑injection surface; although $sess is generated internally, best practice is to use parameterised queries or escape the value
Row‑count verification uses a regex match '-match "2"' which will also succeed for values like "12" or "20", potentially yielding false‑positive test results
The script exits with 'exit $fail'; if more than 255 failures occur the exit code is truncated on Windows, which could misreport the actual failure count
If Select-String finds no match it returns $null; calling .ToString() on $null throws a terminating error, causing the script to crash instead of reporting a FAIL.
Hard‑coded absolute path to the Python interpreter makes the script non‑portable and will fail on systems where the virtual‑env is located elsewhere.

The script increments $script:fail inside function T but later reports and exits using $fail, which is never updated, causing the script to always report 0 failures and exit with code 0 even when tests fail.
Pass/fail counters displayed use $pass and $fail variables that are never modified (the function updates $script:pass/$script:fail), so the summary always shows 0/0 regardless of actual results.
Setting $ErrorActionPreference to 'Continue' suppresses terminating errors, allowing the script to proceed after failures and potentially masking problems; 'Stop' would be safer for a test harness.
A bearer token is hard‑coded in the script, exposing a secret value in source control.

The script overwrites the KRITICAL_DECISION_LOG_ROOT environment variable without preserving its original value; on teardown it simply removes the variable, permanently discarding any pre‑existing setting in the host session.

The test builds the expected file path using UTC date while the module may store files using the local date (or a different format). This mismatch can cause intermittent false‑negative failures, especially around midnight UTC.

An empty KRITICAL_LOGGER_TARGET environment variable is left in the environment after the script finishes; it is not cleared in the teardown, potentially influencing subsequent PowerShell processes that rely on this variable.
